claims.claim: CI must enforce that `package.json` `dependencies` field is empty/absent and that no `node:`/runtime-specific imports exist outside `src/adapter/**` (excepting the documented context-storage case).
claims.rationale: Mechanical enforcement so reviewers don't need judgment to catch violations.
claims.excerpt: any non-empty `dependencies` field fails CI; any `node:` import outside `src/adapter/**` (with the `node:async_hooks` exception) fails a grep-based lint.

claims.claim: Core code must be built entirely on Web Standard APIs (Request, Response, Headers, URL, fetch, ReadableStream, TextEncoder/Decoder, Web Crypto, AbortController, Blob, FormData) with no runtime-specific globals or imports.
claims.rationale: Runtime-specific imports in core cause the framework to fail to load on Cloudflare Workers and silently pull polyfills on Deno; only the WHATWG Fetch standard is common across all nine supported runtimes.
claims.excerpt: Core code is built entirely on Web Standard APIs. Permitted primitives: Request, Response, Headers, URL, URLSearchParams, fetch, ReadableStream / WritableStream / TransformStream, TextEncoder / TextDecoder, globalThis.crypto (Web Crypto), AbortController / AbortSignal, Blob, FormData. No runtime-specific globals or imports.

claims.claim: Core uses a single `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` entry point that runs unmodified on any supported runtime.
claims.rationale: Because core only uses Web Standards, the same fetch signature works everywhere; adapters produce that call.
claims.excerpt: Single `fetch` entry point. Because core only traffics in Web Standards, the same `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` signature works everywhere.

claims.claim: Middleware in `src/middleware/**` must prefer Web Standards; runtime-specific imports are only allowed if cross-runtime in practice.
claims.rationale: Maintains portability of middleware across all supported runtimes.
claims.excerpt: Middleware in `src/middleware/**` must prefer Web Standards; where a runtime-specific import is unavoidable it must be cross-runtime in practice

claims.claim: Node-specific adapter code must stay outside core in the separate `@hono/node-server` package, not in `src/adapter/`.
claims.rationale: Adding it to src/adapter/ would pull Node types into the main hono package and affect every non-Node user's type resolution.
claims.excerpt: `@hono/node-server` is a separate package even though Node is a supported runtime, because adding it to `src/adapter/` would pull Node types into the main `hono` package and affect every non-Node user's type resolution.

claims.claim: Per-adapter helpers may legitimately import runtime primitives (e.g., node:fs/promises in bun adapter, node:path in deno adapter, node:crypto in lambda-edge adapter).
claims.rationale: These imports only execute on runtimes that provide those modules.
claims.excerpt: Per-adapter helpers legitimately import runtime primitives. `src/adapter/bun/serve-static.ts` imports `node:fs/promises`; `src/adapter/deno/serve-static.ts` imports `node:path`; `src/adapter/lambda-edge/handler.ts` imports `node:crypto`.

claims.claim: Runtime-specific integration must live in `src/adapter/<runtime>/**`, translating native event/request shapes into a Web Standard Request, calling app.fetch(request, env, ctx), and shaping the Response back.
claims.rationale: Isolates runtime-specific code from core so the same app.fetch signature works everywhere; the adapter's job is to produce that call, not modify app behavior.
claims.excerpt: Runtime-specific integration lives in `src/adapter/<runtime>/**`. Each adapter translates the runtime's native event/request shape into a Web Standard `Request`, invokes `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` on core

claims.claim: The `hono` package must have zero runtime dependencies; only devDependencies are permitted.
claims.rationale: Runtime deps may not exist on all targeted runtimes, bloat bundles, and expand the supply chain; users install `hono` expecting portability and zero bundle cost.
claims.excerpt: The `hono` package has zero runtime dependencies. `package.json` lists only `devDependencies`. New dependencies in the `dependencies` field are rejected.

claims.claim: The only sanctioned `node:` import in middleware is `node:async_hooks` for AsyncLocalStorage in context-storage; future `node:` imports in middleware require explicit ADR-level justification.
claims.rationale: AsyncLocalStorage is now supported by Node, Bun, Deno, and Cloudflare Workers, making this exception cross-runtime in practice; the exception is not open-ended.
claims.excerpt: the single current example is `node:async_hooks` for `AsyncLocalStorage` in `src/middleware/context-storage/index.ts`, now supported by Node, Bun, Deno, and Cloudflare Workers

claims.claim: Third-party middleware that requires dependencies (Zod, OpenAPI, Sentry, Prisma) must ship as separate `@hono/*` packages outside this repo.
claims.rationale: Keeps deps opt-in so they don't affect users who don't install them; preserves the zero-dep guarantee of core.
claims.excerpt: Middleware that needs deps (Zod validation, OpenAPI generation, Sentry, Prisma integration) ships under `@hono/*` scope or user-owned packages.

claims.claim: Utilities like cookie parsing, query parsing, MIME detection, base64, JWT, UUID, and body reading are hand-implemented in `src/utils/**` rather than delegated to libraries.
claims.rationale: Deliberate cost of the zero-dep rule; needed to keep hono/tiny under 12 kB and respect the Workers 1 MB compressed bundle limit.
claims.excerpt: Utilities are hand-implemented. Functionality that other frameworks delegate to libraries — cookie parsing, query parsing, MIME detection, base64, JWT signing/verification, UUID, body reading — lives in `src/utils/**`.

claims.claim: Consumers of router output must handle both Result<T> representations: [[T, ParamIndexMap][], ParamStash] and [[T, Params][]].
claims.rationale: The dual shape is part of the public contract; each router may choose whichever representation suits it.
claims.excerpt: Implementations may pick whichever representation suits their algorithm; consumers of router output must handle both.

claims.claim: The ParamIndexMap+ParamStash shape avoids eagerly constructing per-request Params objects; routers that don't benefit return the simpler Params shape.
claims.rationale: Explains the rationale for the dual Result<T> shape as a performance optimization.
claims.excerpt: `ParamIndexMap` + `ParamStash` avoids eagerly constructing per-request `Params` objects for every matched route

claims.claim: Consumers such as compose(), context construction, and app.routes introspection must handle both Result<T> representations.
claims.rationale: The dual Result shape is part of the public contract; assuming one form breaks when the active router uses the other.
claims.excerpt: Anything consuming router output (`compose()`, context construction, `app.routes` introspection) must handle both representations.

claims.claim: Users may bypass presets by passing any Router<T> directly to the Hono constructor.
claims.rationale: The Router interface is a public API escape hatch for custom router implementations.
claims.excerpt: Advanced users may bypass presets by passing any `Router<T>` directly: `new Hono({ router: new RegExpRouter() })`

claims.claim: All router implementations must implement the full Router<T> interface (name, add, match).
claims.rationale: A shared contract is required for presets, SmartRouter, and user-supplied routers to work interchangeably.
claims.excerpt: All router implementations implement `Router<T>` defined in `src/router.ts`

claims.claim: Code reading router.name must tolerate SmartRouter's name changing on first match (e.g., 'SmartRouter' → 'SmartRouter + RegExpRouter').
claims.rationale: SmartRouter mutates itself on first match; tests and introspection must handle this transition.
claims.excerpt: `router.name` changes from `"SmartRouter"` to e.g. `"SmartRouter + RegExpRouter"`. Tests, dev tools, and introspection code that read `router.name` must tolerate this transition.

claims.claim: Documentation must flag which path shapes are universally supported across all presets, since runtime behavior may differ between presets for the same app.
claims.rationale: PatternRouter relies on the platform's URLPattern, which has different semantics than RegExpRouter+TrieRouter.
claims.excerpt: Documentation must flag which path shapes are universally supported across all presets.

claims.claim: Each router must be covered by benchmarks; removing routers or presets without benchmark evidence is not supported.
claims.rationale: The multi-router strategy requires comparative data to keep presets' rationale valid.
claims.excerpt: Removing routers or presets without benchmark evidence is not a supported maintenance path.

claims.claim: LinearRouter does near-zero work at build time and walks routes in order at match time; ideal for short-lived workers with modest route counts.
claims.rationale: Background on router tradeoffs for edge/cold-start scenarios.
claims.excerpt: LinearRouter does near-zero work at build time and walks routes in order at match time. Ideal for very short-lived workers

claims.claim: New routers must support the full path grammar accepted by at least one existing router.
claims.rationale: Otherwise a preset swap would silently change which routes work, a breakage undetectable without enumerating every path pattern.
claims.excerpt: New routers must support the full path grammar, including every path shape that at least one existing router already accepts.

claims.claim: Partial router implementations (e.g., throwing on certain HTTP methods or not supporting path patterns other routers accept) are not allowed.
claims.rationale: Partial implementations would break preset swaps and the shared contract.
claims.excerpt: Partial implementations (e.g., a router that throws on certain HTTP methods, or doesn't support a subset of path patterns the other routers accept) are not allowed.

claims.claim: PatternRouter wraps the platform's URLPattern, shipping the smallest code because the regex engine is the runtime's.
claims.rationale: Background explaining why hono/tiny minimizes bundle size.
claims.excerpt: PatternRouter wraps the platform's `URLPattern` — it ships the smallest code because the regex engine is the runtime's, not the framework's.

claims.claim: Presets are separate module graphs, not runtime configuration flags, so bundlers ship only the code for the chosen preset.
claims.rationale: Bundle size optimization depends on tree-shaking at the module level; runtime flags would defeat this.
claims.excerpt: they're different module graphs so the bundler ships different code

claims.claim: RegExpRouter compiles routes into one combined regex for fastest matching but throws UnsupportedPathError on unsupported path shapes and has high build cost.
claims.rationale: Background on router tradeoffs informing the multi-router strategy.
claims.excerpt: RegExpRouter compiles all routes into one combined regex for fast matching, but throws `UnsupportedPathError` on path shapes it can't encode and pays a non-trivial up-front build cost.

claims.claim: Removing a preset entry point is a breaking change; adding new presets is additive and safe.
claims.rationale: Preset choice is a public API commitment downstream users import from.
claims.excerpt: Removing `hono/quick` or `hono/tiny` is a breaking change for every downstream user importing from those paths.

claims.claim: SmartRouter tries each router in order on first match, catches UnsupportedPathError, locks to the first successful router, and rebinds its own match method to that router.
claims.rationale: This enables paying router selection cost only once while avoiding a hard choice between routers.
claims.excerpt: On first `match()`, it tries each router in order, catches `UnsupportedPathError`, locks to the first router that succeeds, and hot-swaps its own `match` method to the winner's bound method

claims.claim: The Router<T> interface is public API; breaking changes to it are breaking changes to user code.
claims.rationale: Users implement Router<T> for custom routers, so the interface is their API, not just internal.
claims.excerpt: The interface is their API, not just an internal abstraction. Breaking changes to the interface are breaking changes to user code.

claims.claim: Three preset entry points ship: hono (SmartRouter[RegExpRouter, TrieRouter]), hono/quick (SmartRouter[LinearRouter, TrieRouter]), and hono/tiny (PatternRouter alone).
claims.rationale: Each preset is optimized for a different deployment target (long-running servers, edge/cold-start, minimum bundle).
claims.excerpt: Three presets ship as separate entry points

claims.claim: TrieRouter supports every path shape Hono allows but is slower per match than RegExpRouter.
claims.rationale: Background on router tradeoffs.
claims.excerpt: TrieRouter supports every path shape Hono allows but is slower per match than RegExpRouter.

claims.claim: Users needing fully deterministic behavior should pass a concrete router instead of a SmartRouter-based preset.
claims.rationale: SmartRouter's self-mutation on first match means its identity is not fully deterministic.
claims.excerpt: Users needing fully deterministic behavior should pass a concrete router instead of a SmartRouter-based preset.

claims.claim: Bug fixes in compose.ts must verify the one-handler fast-path still works, and vice versa
claims.rationale: Two distinct dispatch paths exist that can diverge
claims.excerpt: Fixing a bug in `compose.ts` without verifying the one-handler path still works (or vice versa) is a trap.

claims.claim: Errors thrown in the chain are caught by compose() and delegated to the app's onError handler
claims.rationale: Defines error propagation behavior in the middleware chain
claims.excerpt: Errors thrown anywhere in the chain are caught by `compose()` and delegated to the app's `onError` handler (`src/compose.ts:52-59`).

claims.claim: Forgetting await on next() is a silent bug class not caught by types or compose()
claims.rationale: Non-awaited next() causes downstream handlers to run concurrently with continuation, violating the onion model
claims.excerpt: Middleware that calls `next()` without `await` will let downstream handlers run concurrently with its own continuation, violating the onion model.

claims.claim: Hono does not support Fastify-style encapsulation; scope-local lifecycle plugins must be rebuilt as middleware composition
claims.rationale: Accepted trade-off for a simpler model that composes across runtimes without a plugin manager
claims.excerpt: **The Koa model locks Hono out of Fastify-style encapsulation.** Plugins that rely on scope-local lifecycle... must be rebuilt in terms of middleware composition.

claims.claim: Hono uses Koa-style onion middleware composition implemented in src/compose.ts
claims.rationale: Foundational middleware model choice; wrapping logic is natural and errors propagate via promise rejection
claims.excerpt: Hono uses **Koa-style onion composition**. The implementation is `src/compose.ts` — 74 lines modeled on `koa-compose` with additions for `onError` and `onNotFound` handlers.

claims.claim: If a handler returns nothing and c.finalized is false at the tail, the onNotFound handler runs
claims.rationale: Defines not-found fallback behavior
claims.excerpt: If a handler returns nothing and `c.finalized` is false at the tail of the chain, the `onNotFound` handler runs (`src/compose.ts:62-65`).

claims.claim: Middleware must call await next() at most once; calling next() twice throws 'next() called multiple times'
claims.rationale: Prevents bugs where middleware accidentally advances the chain twice
claims.excerpt: calling `next()` twice triggers `'next() called multiple times'` (`src/compose.ts:33-35`)

claims.claim: Middleware signature is (c: Context, next: Next) => Promise<Response | void>
claims.rationale: Defines the contract all middleware must implement
claims.excerpt:
type MiddlewareHandler<E, P, I> = (
  c: Context<E, P, I>,
  next: Next
) => Promise<Response | void>

claims.claim: Routes with exactly one matched handler bypass compose() and are invoked directly via a fast-path in src/hono-base.ts:424-442
claims.rationale: Performance optimization to save closure/promise/microtask allocations on edge deploys
claims.excerpt: routes that matched exactly one handler do NOT flow through `compose()`**. The dispatch code in `src/hono-base.ts:424-442` detects `matchResult[0].length === 1` and invokes the single handler directly

claims.claim: Routes with two or more handlers go through compose() normally
claims.rationale: Only single-handler routes take the fast path
claims.excerpt: Routes with two or more handlers (middleware + terminal handler, or any chain) go through `compose()` normally.

claims.claim: Tests exercising middleware composition must use routes with at least two handlers to actually invoke compose()
claims.rationale: Single-handler routes skip compose() entirely via the fast-path
claims.excerpt: Tests that exercise middleware composition MUST have at least two handlers in the route; otherwise they don't exercise `compose()` at all.

claims.claim: The single-handler fast-path is an invariant and must not be removed as duplicated logic
claims.rationale: Fast-path is not equivalent to compose() with one middleware because compose() allocates closures and promises unconditionally
claims.excerpt: **The fast-path is an invariant, not an optimization.** Removing it "because it duplicates compose logic" is a subtle regression

claims.claim: compose.ts is high blast radius; changes should include micro-benchmarks in review
claims.rationale: Every 2+ handler route on every deployment flows through this file; regressions affect the entire ecosystem
claims.excerpt: Reviews of `compose.ts` changes should include micro-benchmarks.

claims.claim: compose.ts should remain small and auditable (~74 lines)
claims.rationale: Fitting on one screen enables diagnosing dispatch bugs by reading the whole file
claims.excerpt: **Small file = auditable.** `compose.ts` is 74 lines... The fact that it fits on one screen is itself a feature

claims.claim: onError is per-compose-invocation, not global; mounted sub-apps with a non-default errorHandler are wrapped so their errors are caught by their own handler
claims.rationale: Mounted apps maintain their own error-handling identity
claims.excerpt: **`onError` is per-compose-invocation**, not global. `src/hono-base.ts:208-232` wires this up for nested apps

claims.claim: Path patterns must propagate into handler Context via `MergePath<BasePath, P>`, including contributions from `.basePath()` and `.route()` mounts.
claims.rationale: This makes `c.req.param('<name>')` typed from the pattern string across nested apps.
claims.excerpt: `MergePath<BasePath, P>` carries the route pattern into `Context<E, P, I>`

claims.claim: Alternatives (OpenAPI codegen, runtime schema introspection, explicit route type annotations) were rejected due to build-step drift, runtime bundle cost, and duplication.
claims.rationale: Documents why the no-codegen, type-only approach was chosen over alternatives.
claims.excerpt: Alternatives considered: - **Code generation** (OpenAPI → client): adds a build step, drifts from the runtime schema

claims.claim: Backwards-incompatible changes to `validator()`'s Input production belong in major version bumps with migration guidance.
claims.rationale: Third-party validator adapters depend on the `validator()` type signature and breaking it cascades across the ecosystem.
claims.excerpt: Backwards-incompatible changes to how `validator()` produces `Input` break every adapter validator in the ecosystem. These changes belong in major version bumps with migration guidance.

claims.claim: Each route method must be typed via `HandlerInterface` with overloads for every handler-chain arity, and adding route-level features requires extending overloads for every arity.
claims.rationale: Inconsistent overload sets cause subtle inference collapses; adding features to only single-handler overloads breaks multi-handler variants.
claims.excerpt: Adding a route-level feature ... that sits between `.get(path, ...)` and the handler MUST extend `HandlerInterface` overloads for every arity.

claims.claim: Internal helper bodies may use `any` where necessary for types to compose.
claims.rationale: Some compositions require internal type escapes (e.g., `this as any`) even while the declared surface remains precise.
claims.excerpt: Internal helper bodies may use `any` where necessary for the types to compose (e.g., `this as any` returns in `src/hono-base.ts:139`).

claims.claim: Middleware authors modifying `MiddlewareHandler` generics must preserve `Input` propagation.
claims.rationale: Seemingly local changes to generic defaults or parameter order can silently break validator→handler inference for all users.
claims.excerpt: Middleware authors touching `MiddlewareHandler` generics ... must preserve `Input` propagation.

claims.claim: Response-shape changes are accepted as compile-time breaking changes for all typed `hc` clients.
claims.rationale: Typed clients mean the types are the API; breaking changes surface at client compilation rather than at runtime.
claims.excerpt: `hc<typeof app>` makes response-shape changes compile-time breaking changes for every `hc` consumer

claims.claim: Route registration must return a HonoBase whose Schema accumulates losslessly as `S & ToSchema<M, P, I, MergeTypedResponse<R>>`.
claims.rationale: The accumulated generic `S` is what `hc<typeof app>` consumes; losing accumulation degrades the client type surface.
claims.excerpt: so that `app.get(...).post(...).route(...)` chains accumulate a composite `Schema` losslessly.

claims.claim: The Proxy `get` trap must return `undefined` for the `then` property to prevent auto-awaiting as a thenable.
claims.rationale: Without this guard the Proxy collapses into a resolved promise; this runtime invariant isn't captured by types.
claims.excerpt: The `get` trap returns `undefined` for the `then` property ... specifically to prevent the Proxy from being auto-awaited as a thenable

claims.claim: The type-inference chain from route registration through handler Context to typed client must be preserved end-to-end across all four segments (route schema accumulation, path propagation, validator Input flow, client inference).
claims.rationale: Type inference is Hono's marketed differentiator; silent degradation to `any` is the worst failure mode because it produces no compile error but loses autocomplete and type safety.
claims.excerpt: The type-inference chain from route registration through handler Context to typed client MUST be preserved end-to-end.

claims.claim: The typed RPC client runtime is implemented as a recursive JavaScript Proxy in `createProxy`, with no build step or runtime schema dependency.
claims.rationale: Keeps client bundle tiny; coupling between server and client is only the type-level `typeof app` import.
claims.excerpt: The runtime implementation is a recursive JavaScript `Proxy` — 17 lines in `src/client/client.ts:15-31`

claims.claim: The validator's `Input` must merge into the downstream handler's Context so `c.req.valid(target)` returns the validated type.
claims.rationale: Breaking Input propagation through middleware composition silently breaks the validator→handler inference chain.
claims.excerpt: the validator's `Input` merges into the downstream handler's `Context<E, P, I>` so `c.req.valid(target)` returns the validated type.

claims.claim: Type-only tests (expectTypeOf, Expect<Equal<A,B>>) must be preserved; removing them is equivalent to removing runtime regression tests.
claims.rationale: Type-level tests are the mechanism that fails CI when the inference chain breaks.
claims.excerpt: Removing them to "clean up" the test suite is the same class of mistake as removing runtime regression tests.

claims.claim: Typed RPC client must be derived from `typeof app` with no code generation step; there is no fallback to codegen.
claims.rationale: Codegen adds build steps and allows drift between runtime schema and client types; the no-codegen commitment is architectural, not a performance optimization.
claims.excerpt: No codegen is a hard architectural commitment, not a performance optimization.

claims.claim: `any` is not allowed in the declared type surface (route method return types, Context generics, HandlerInterface overloads, hc return types, validator output types).
claims.rationale: Internal helper bodies may use `any` for composition, but API-surface `any` defeats the inference chain.
claims.excerpt: `any` at the API surface is never tolerated.

claims.claim: `tsc --noEmit` must remain part of the release test gate.
claims.rationale: Removing type checking from the gate removes the primary protection for the inference chain.
claims.excerpt: Removing the type check from the gate removes the primary protection for the inference chain.

claims.claim: The jsx-runtime.ts/jsx-dev-runtime.ts exported names (jsx, jsxs, jsxDEV, Fragment) are implicit public API; changing them breaks every compiled downstream file.
claims.rationale: TypeScript emits imports from these files automatically when jsxImportSource is configured.
claims.excerpt: Changes to their exported names (`jsx`, `jsxs`, `jsxDEV`, `Fragment`) break every compiled file in every downstream app.

claims.claim: Adding a new rendering backend must be done as a new entry point following the same pattern, not as a runtime option on an existing runtime.
claims.rationale: Architecture is explicitly extensible via additional backends, not via backend flags.
claims.excerpt: requires a third entry point following the same pattern, not a runtime option on an existing runtime

claims.claim: Backend selection is at build time via TypeScript's jsxImportSource, not at runtime.
claims.rationale: Only the selected backend's code is bundled; tree-shaking alone is insufficient because runtimes have different module shapes and side effects.
claims.excerpt: Backend selection is at build time, not runtime.

claims.claim: Changes to the JSX subsystem must be verified against both renderers; either-side-only changes are suspect.
claims.rationale: The JSX subsystem spans 30+ files across both backends and semantic drift is a known risk.
claims.excerpt: Changes must be verified against both renderers; either-side-only changes are suspect.

claims.claim: Cloudflare Workers' 1 MB bundle-size limit is a real constraint motivating separate runtimes.
claims.rationale: Shipping an unused reconciler on the server would exceed Workers size budgets.
claims.excerpt: bundle-size problem on Workers, where the 1 MB limit is real

claims.claim: Element/Fragment representation, createContext/useContext, common types, and Children utilities are shared between both backends.
claims.rationale: Shared core keeps components portable across renderers.
claims.excerpt: Element and Fragment representation (`src/jsx/base.ts`) — both backends consume the same element shape.

claims.claim: Hono ships two JSX runtimes behind distinct entry points (hono/jsx for server, hono/jsx/dom for client) sharing a common element/component model.
claims.rationale: Server and client have fundamentally different rendering needs; a unified runtime would force unused code into each target.
claims.excerpt: Hono ships **two JSX runtimes behind distinct entry points**, sharing a common element/component model

claims.claim: Hook implementations are duplicated between server and client; attempts to unify hooks with legitimately different semantics are discouraged.
claims.rationale: Hook semantics diverge between server and client; unifying them produces subtle bugs.
claims.excerpt: attempts to unify hook implementations that have legitimately different semantics will produce subtle bugs

claims.claim: Importing DOM-specific hooks (e.g., useEffect from hono/jsx/dom) into server-executing code pulls DOM code into the server bundle and frequently fails at runtime.
claims.rationale: Build configs must route jsxImportSource per-target to keep bundles correct.
claims.excerpt: Importing `useEffect` from `hono/jsx/dom` in code that also runs server-side pulls DOM hook code into the server bundle and frequently fails at runtime.

claims.claim: React-compatibility is a feature of hono/jsx/dom only; the server runtime does not commit to matching React's server semantics.
claims.rationale: Apps doing both SSR and hydration must work within the intersection of both runtimes' APIs.
claims.excerpt: React-compatibility is a feature of `hono/jsx/dom`, not `hono/jsx`.

claims.claim: Shared isomorphic components cannot use features unique to one runtime side.
claims.rationale: Must work within the intersection of both runtimes' APIs.
claims.excerpt: features unique to one side cannot be used by shared components

claims.claim: Stateful client hooks like useState and useEffect are not meaningful on the server runtime and are either no-ops or unavailable.
claims.rationale: There is no commit phase or DOM on the server; client hook semantics don't apply.
claims.excerpt: Stateful client hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) are not meaningful here and are either no-ops or unavailable.

claims.claim: Tests must cover both renderers for any shared primitive to prevent semantic drift between server and client implementations.
claims.rationale: Changes to one renderer without matching the other silently breaks isomorphic components.
claims.excerpt: Tests MUST cover both renderers for any shared primitive.

claims.claim: The client-side runtime provides DOM mounting, hydration, reconciler, scheduler, and the full hook suite with React-compatibility shims.
claims.rationale: Describes the client runtime's feature set and its compatibility goal.
claims.excerpt: DOM mounting, hydration, reconciler, scheduler, full hook suite

claims.claim: The server-side JSX runtime provides string-streaming SSR, Suspense with boundary placeholders, async components, and ErrorBoundary.
claims.rationale: Describes the server runtime's feature set.
claims.excerpt: String-streaming SSR; `<Suspense>` streaming with boundary placeholders; async components; `ErrorBoundary`.

claims.claim: hono/jsx, hono/jsx/dom, and hono/jsx/dom/client are public entry points; removing or restructuring them is a breaking change.
claims.rationale: Downstream users depend on these as stable build-configuration contracts.
claims.excerpt: Removing or restructuring them is a breaking change for every downstream user.
